data.title: NAD: Entirely Normal Return, We Can Do Better In Munis
data.description:
How machines work matters

The internal workings of a machine - how the cogs and axles and so on fit together - determines the output of the machine. So, if we want to predict the output of the machine we need to look at those cogs and axles and see what they do.

Financial instruments are themselves machines. They work according to the rules of their components. Thus, in order to predict the output of the financial instrument we should first understand those parts themselves, then their interactions.

This does entail a certain amount of boring attention to detail - but we're talking about money here, right, so that's OK.

Nuveen Quality Muni Income Fund (NAD)

I'd not say there's anything wrong with this fund at all. It's perfectly straightforward, does what it says on the tin and so on. The return is similarly perfectly straightforward and runs in the middle of the pack of the funds that do the same thing.

My complaint is that I don't think the strategy being employed is correct. Or, perhaps, that there's a better strategy in this section of the market. To understand this point we do, sadly, need those details of the bond market. Once we've got those it's possible to explain my point.

To repeat though - I don't think NAD is bad or wrong. I just prefer a different underlying strategy.

Bond basics

There's a full and detailed explanation here starting at "Bond Basics".

The lessons to learn from that are that there are two risks on bonds - credit risk and interest rate risk. To beat credit risk we should almost certainly be in a fund, interest rate risk is something we can avoid but cannot beat. All bond funds are trading off those two - take more of them and the recorded distribution or interest payout rises. But, at the costs of those risks. Take fewer of those risks and the distribution will fall.

What we really want is to be cute and to be able to gain the higher payout without having to take that extra risk. Difficult but sometimes possible at least so say I.

Tax equivalent yields

Muni bonds are taxfree, which gives us a problem in evaluating them. The way we get around this is to talk of tax equivalent yields. What would a taxable investment have to pay to give the same, final, cash in hand return as this tax free one? As above, this depends upon the tax rate of the investor but fortunately people publish tables of this for us:

How the pushing down of yields from the wall of money plus the tax breaks works out is that the lower returns aren't worth it if you're not paying income tax and they're very definitely worth thinking about if you're a top rate taxpayer still looking for income. The breakpoint in the middle is usually put at the 24% marginal (ie, the tax rate on the next $ of income) rate. Below that possibly not, above possibly so. Given how people differ in their love/hatred of having to pay tax it's not possible to be more accurate than that.

Bond basics

Municipal bonds give a tax free income. Longer tenor bonds give a higher coupon at the price of more interest rate risk. Higher credit quality bonds give a lower income with less credit risk - and vice versa for lower credit bonds. The risk of default in munis is really pretty low but not nothing so a bond fund for diversification can be justified.

How does NAD do it

Near all - not quite all, but near - muni bond funds are selecting their strategy from within these simple little cogs. Trading off tenor and income against interest rate risk, trading off credit risk and income. There aren't that many levers to pull if we're honest about it.

Now of course tactics is something else. There's no one solid count for the number of muni bonds in existence and even if there were it would be different by the next market trading day. But tens of thousands at least. Everyone is picking and choosing among those tens of thousands. Even "index" funds - and most certainly those shadowing an index - are holding only a portion of all issues.

So we have the bond fund strategy - credit risk, tenor, interest rate risk - and we also have tactics of which specific issues shall we hold to match that strategy?

My argument against NAD is not that their tactics are bad - far from it, they produce a usual and normal return from the one they follow. Their tactics are good that is. I just think it's the wrong strategy to be using in munis.

So the actual details of what NAD is doing:

Overall they run with near 1200 positions (1,174 in fact at my date) which is pretty well diversified. There are funds out there running with 300 positions for example.

They're going for pretty solid credit quality (not much of the muni market is AAA rated) and as a guess - I insist, a guess, not a proof - I'd say their holdings of B and CCC are issues which have declined in quality since issue. But, as up at the top, high credit quality means a more moderate income or coupon.

They're going pretty long maturity in search of yield on the other hand. This increases interest rate risk of course. Something we can backcheck here:

As interest rates rose after lockdown the fund lost near 40% of its capital value. This is also what happened to near all long tenor bond funds - that's just what interest rate risk means.

Fees are 0.94% plus interest costs - pretty standard for the form of the fund.

NAD is a closed end fund

An open ended fund - like an exchange traded fund, ETF - takes our money when we buy and increases the investment in the fund. This also means that when we sell they have to pay us out from the fund. Of course, there's a cash margin to handle this. But it can happen that a lot, a lot, of us all sell at the same time. So, investments must be sold to pay us out. If the underlying investments are illiquid this can mean selling at bad prices, which spooks more selling and we get a redemption cascade. This is like a bank run and no one wants to be the last in line at one of those. This also isn't going to happen in muni bonds.

The other type of fund is a closed end one. Here, we pay the selling shareholder - our investment doesn't flow in or out of the fund itself. Runs cannot happen. But two more things can. Firstly, the fund can borrow and most do. Here, NAD is 41% leveraged which is about where most get to, around 40%. The insight is that the interest to pay on the short term money borrowed (and then reborrowed, the loans are rolled over) is lower than the coupons on the bonds held. That same longer term coupons are higher than shorter we mentioned with interest rate risk. So, leverage juices returns at the addition of some risk - in true disasters those loans have to be paid back at par which isn't true of us investors.

The other thing is that the market price of a CEF can move well off net asset value. As has happened to NAD:

The traditional cure for a discount like this is to make "return of capital" payments. It's possible to argue about how this works, one is that this reduces the net asset value to market price and that's that. Another is that unobservant, possibly naive, investors see the high yield and buy, pushing up the market price to NAV. Which, well, yes. I'd argue that both definitely happen but which is dominant, well, sorry, not one of those I can answer.

ROC isn't a problem anyway, as long as we know about it - for two reasons. Firstly, we have to adjust for it to get to the "true" earnings from the fund. As a general, but not perfect, rule it doesn't matter which calculation of earnings or income we use as long as we use the same one across the instruments we're trying to compare. So, we must adjust for RoC when comparing with funds that aren't trying to close an NAV gap. The second is that we have to know so that we don't go and spend that money - RoC isn't income, it's capital, so needs to be reinvested in order to maintain our capital position.

So, what does NAD get us?

7.24% certainly looks good. Woot even! Ah, but it isn't. 35% of that is return of capital (trailing yields, as here, come from what was paid, not what will be). So, to get that "true" earnings number we need 7.24 x 0.65 which is: 4.7%.

Which is OK for a muni fund following this strategy. Nuveen aren't being bad at their tactics that is.

My complaint is different

It's the strategy I differ with, not the details of day to day operation here. As I pointed out before:

Now "not rated" in the bond markets usually means something like "complete dog and not touching it with your bargepole let alone mine". CCC isn't much better. Actually, in large parts of the bond market, BBB isn't thought of as much better than that.

At which point we could just assume that SHYM is achieving high yield by taking on lots of credit risk-the standard complaint is that we can only get paid in financial markets by taking risk.

However, the actual default rate in the muni markets is really very low-that 0.1% estimate above. So we could be dealing with comparative credit worthiness, not actual. Sure, a BBB muni is less creditworthy than an AAA but if the overall default rate is tiny, how much difference does that actually make?

Also, not rating means that the issuer hasn't paid a rating agency to rate the bond. This is not uncommon for small issues either. Fund managers can still decide upon their own opinion by having their own look at the finances of the issuer. That is, "unrated" does not mean "not worthy of a rating" it means "no one has paid for a rating" which is a very different thing.

Effectively, a fund heavily into unrated bonds is using the calculations of its own managers rather than the calculations of the rating agencies. Many funds won't touch unrated bonds, so there's a possibility that they're underpriced. Maybe, possibly, we cannot be categorical about this.

That, I think, is the special secret sauce here. Grasping that unrated in the muni market does not mean high credit risk, even if unrated (and other low-rating bonds) pay higher coupons. Thus, without a (substantial that is) increase in risk, it is possible to gain a higher income. Which is the great trick in any financial market-to get paid for taking risks you're not actually taking or to be overpaid for the ones you are.

I approve of this-and again, it's not a trick; it's an insight-and strategy.

That low - 0.1% - default rate in munis means I think credit risk at the low end is overpriced. Therefore, we're getting paid for a risk we're not taking.

Thus I end up saying that we can take - or leave - interest rate risk as we're comfortable with. But we should load up on nominal credit risk in munis (note, this does not apply to other markets!). Thus I'm fine with long or short tenor muni funds but think we should be in low credit rating, not high, ones.

Why I can be wrong

Obviously I can be wrong - no, really, it is possible! The two most obvious reasons here are that, firstly, many investors in munis are people looking for an income they can actually live on - for decades. Risk, credit risk, is simply an anathema. Of course, that also contributes to the mispricing that we can take advantage of but for those very risk-averse investors my thoughts are wrong.

The other is that we've not had a proper recession for a long, long time. No, 2008 wasn't a recession, that was a financial crisis, no lockdown wasn't a recession that was a lockdown. How well municipal finances will hold up - and thus the default rate - in a real and proper recession is unknown, we've simply not had one for so long.

My opinion

NAD is run perfectly well for the strategy being followed. It's even being run well for that strategy. 4.7% on a high credit rating, long tenor muni bond fund is fine.

On the other hand we can also gain 4.11% in a short tenor (ie, low interest rate risk) but low credit rating fund like iShares Short Duration High Yield Muni Active ETF (SHYM). Or - and from the same managing company - as much as a full percentage point higher than NAD in long tenor, low credit rating munis such as Nuveen Municipal Credit Opps Fund (NMCO) or Nuveen Municipal High Income Opportunity Fund (NMZ).

Because I think credit risk in munis is overpriced I therefore prefer those three to NAD.

data.title: Uranium: The Positive Narrative Is About To Change
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Goehring & Rozencwajg Natural Resource Market Q3 2025 Commentary originally posted on Nov. 26, 2025.

Trump’s $120 bn nuclear deal a fresh blow to uranium short sellers - Bloomberg News, October 2, 2025

Uranium prices continued to move higher in the third quarter. Spot prices rose nearly 12%, ending the period at $81.90 per pound. Term prices, as reported by Cameco (CCJ), also advanced, increasing more than 5% to $82.20. Uranium equities performed strongly as well, gaining 26%. Year-to-date, the URNM ETF (URNM)—the largest uranium-equity vehicle—is up almost 50%, making it the second-best performing equity group in the natural resource sector.

Despite this strong performance, overall investor interest remains subdued, and speculative sentiment continues to lean heavily negative. As the chart below shows, shares outstanding in the URNM ETF have fallen by nearly 15% since the start of the year—a clear indication of declining investor participation.

FIGURE 6 URNM Shares Outstanding

What continues to interest us most is the persistent bearish positioning among hedge funds. In our first-quarter 2025 letter, we noted how short interest in uranium stocks had surged across the hedge fund community, with four of the five most heavily shorted names on the Australian exchange belonging to the uranium sector. Working from faulty assumptions, many funds believed the Sprott Physical Uranium Trust (U.U:CA) would face liquidity problems and eventually be forced to sell large quantities of uranium into a thin spot market, driving prices sharply lower. On that basis, they built substantial short positions in several uranium names.

We argued at the time that Sprott's liquidity issues were temporary and readily addressed, and we predicted that a significant short squeeze could emerge over the summer. That is exactly what happened: the Sprott Uranium Trust raised capital with ease, triggering a major squeeze across the uranium equities.

One might have expected hedge funds to reconsider their approach after that episode, but the evidence suggests otherwise. As the headline above makes clear, funds have again accumulated sizeable short positions in uranium stocks. The article's opening line is telling: "Investors piled into uranium stocks on Wednesday after the US government unveiled a gold $80 bn plan to build nuclear reactors, delivering a near killer blow to hedge funds betting against the sector."

When the uranium bull market finally approaches its top, we have little doubt that sentiment—hedge funds included—will swing to a wildly bullish extreme. That will be the moment to step aside. For now, with speculators firmly entrenched on the bearish side, it is hard to imagine we are anywhere near such a peak. The uranium bull market still has many years ahead of it, and we take considerable comfort not only in the lack of broad investor interest but also in the hedge fund community's persistent skepticism.

Both the short-term and long-term uranium outlook continues to improve. On the demand side, the positive news keeps accumulating—the U.S. government's announcement this week that it intends to support the construction of ten new Westinghouse (BAM:CA) AP1000 reactors is only the latest example. But it is the supply-related developments, in our view, that will drive the next major leg higher in uranium prices.

As we wrote last quarter: “If the demand story is now widely appreciated, the supply story is not—and that may be where the real leverage lies. Several of the world’s key growth projects are wobbling... the market’s set-up is therefore tightening from both ends: a visible swell in long-term demand and quiet erosion of expected supply.”

In the “Uranium” section of this letter, we walk through not only the news supporting demand but also the steadily growing list of negative supply developments. These unexpected supply disappointments will become a major contributor to the uranium market’s emerging structural deficit—an issue to which investors, so far, have paid almost no attention.

Problems Developing With Uranium Supply

Global uranium markets have been awash in bullish developments over the past two years, as governments, utilities, investors, and even long-skeptical environmental groups have begun casting aside the narratives that constrained nuclear power for four decades. After years of stagnation—during which global generating capacity failed to recover from Fukushima—it was only in 2023 that nuclear output finally crept past its 2010 peak. Today, the outlook for nuclear power looks radically different.

In its newly released v , the World Nuclear Association (WNA) lays out a series of projections based on its assessment of global nuclear trends. From a 2025 base of 398 GWe of installed generating capacity, the WNA's base-case scenario now expects capacity to reach 746 GWe by 2040—an increase of roughly 90% from today's levels. Notably, that 746 GWe estimate represents an upward revision of nearly 10% from projections made just two years ago.

The WNA has also raised both its lower- and upper-bound forecasts. Its low-case scenario now calls for 552 GWe by 2040, an increase of 66 GWe over the 2023 outlook. Its high-case scenario has risen to 966 GWe, up 35 GWe from the prior estimate. Even under the WNA's most pessimistic projection, global nuclear capacity is expected to expand by at least 40%.

On the uranium fuel side, the WNA now expects global reactor requirements to total roughly 179.1 million pounds this year. (It is worth noting that this figure sits nearly 3% above the already-bullish 177 million pounds of demand we ourselves projected just four years ago.) Under its base-case outlook, the WNA estimates that reactor demand will reach 330 million pounds by 2040—an extraordinary 90% increase from today's levels.

In its upper-case scenario, the WNA projects reactor demand could rise to 530 million pounds, a truly immense expansion. Even the most conservative projection puts 2040 demand at 278 million pounds—almost 100 million pounds more than current consumption.

Given these enormous growth assumptions, the expansion of uranium supply over the next fifteen years will be critical. And on that front, the challenges are already beginning to surface. The supply problems emerging today will only deepen the structural deficits now developing in global uranium markets.

The uranium market in 2025 has already fallen in deficit when investment demand is included, and we believe uranium markets next year will slip into outright operating deficit. We estimated uranium mine supply in 2025 will reach 160 mm pounds, secondary supply will approximately 25 mm pounds, and investment demand will approach 10 mm pounds—producing a deficit of 5 mm pounds. For 2026 we estimate both uranium mine supply to be down and reactor demand to be up, producing an outright deficit in global uranium market before investment demand is factored in.

Problems are emerging on several fronts. In Kazakhstan, the eastern anchor of global uranium supply, Kazatomprom (NATKY)—the world's largest producer—continues to struggle with its production expansion plans. After the sharp rebound in uranium demand in 2022, the company announced an ambitious target: an 11-million-pound increase in output, roughly a 20% rise. Then, in September 2023, Kazatomprom went further, outlining plans to lift 2025 production by an additional 12 million pounds, potentially bringing total output to 80 million pounds by utilizing 100% of the subsoil exploitation rights granted by the Kazakh government.

We met with Kazatomprom in Almaty in June 2024 and left with the clear impression that the company's production guidance would need to be revised downward. Earlier that year, in a surprise announcement, Kazatomprom had already cut its 2024 production estimate by 10 million pounds. Yet at the time of our visit, management continued to maintain its 80-million-pound production target for 2025.

Much of the blame for the 2024 reduction had been attributed to shortages of sulfuric acid. But in our view, far deeper production issues were embedded in the company's massive Budenovskoye 6 and 7 greenfield developments—projects beset by both political complications and geological difficulties.

Original plans called for the Budenovskoye projects to produce 13 million pounds of uranium by the end of 2025. After our visit, we came away with the strong conviction that this target was unattainable—a view we explored in detail in our essay “ Uranium: A Drama in the Making ,” which is readily accessible online.

In a development that stunned the uranium analytical community, Kazatomprom announced in September 2024 that it was cutting its 2025 production forecast from 80 million pounds to 69 million—a reduction of more than 10 million pounds.

The company has offered little insight into what is happening at Budenovskoye, but it is difficult to escape the conclusion that the bulk of the shortfall stems from organizational and construction delays at the project.

The disappointments have not stopped there. On August 22, the company revealed that it was reducing its 2026 subsoil use agreement with the Kazakh government from 32,777 tonnes to 29,697 tonnes—a 10% cut. Kazatomprom also noted that it is highly likely to exercise its “down-flex” option within the allowable 20% deviation under its newly lowered 2026 production framework. In practical terms, the company now expects to produce only 62 million pounds of uranium in 2026—five million pounds less than the reduced 2025 level.

Only a few years ago, Kazatomprom was projecting that it would produce 80 million pounds of uranium in 2025, and that, by utilizing 100% of its 32,777-tonne subsoil-use rights from the Kazakh government, 2026 production would rise to roughly 85 million pounds. Today, however, it appears that 2026 output will reach only 62 million pounds—a massive reduction from the 85-million-pound figure once presented as achievable.

In its first-half 2025 financial release, the company attributed most of the 10% production downgrade to “production adjustments” at Budenovskoye. Kazatomprom also noted that financing had finally been secured for an 800,000-tonne sulfuric acid plant—critical for the viability of the project—and that construction was under way on additional Budenovskoye processing facilities expected to add 6,000 tonnes of annual capacity. No timeline was provided for completion; the press release offered only that “construction ... is progressing in accordance with its schedule.”

In earlier letters, we discussed the problems at Budenovskoye and the central role the project was expected to play in closing the emerging structural gap between global uranium supply and demand. It now appears that much of the project’s originally envisioned 15-million-pound annual output may never materialize.

Uranium supply problems are not confined to the eastern hemisphere. Cameco—the largest uranium producer in the West—has also been forced to trim its 2025 production guidance for the McArthur River mine, one of the world’s premier uranium assets. The company had originally projected that McArthur River would produce 18 million pounds of uranium in 2025. However, due to development delays and slower-than-expected progress on ground-freezing operations, Cameco has lowered its forecast to between 14 and 15 million pounds.

Cameco has not indicated whether these issues will affect its 2026 production, and it is a development we will continue to watch closely. But the implications are clear: production setbacks at McArthur River add yet another data point to the growing evidence that supply constraints will only widen the uranium market’s emerging structural deficit in the years ahead.

Finally, we believe it is imperative to address one potentially significant risk to future supply. One of the largest sources of new uranium expected to enter the market between now and 2030 is NexGen (NXE)’s Arrow/Rook I project in Saskatchewan. The mine has advanced almost completely through Canada’s multilayered permitting process. Provincial approval was granted in November 2023. The critical federal Environmental Impact Statement was accepted as final in January 2025.

Only one approval now remains before construction can begin: authorization from the Canadian Nuclear Safety Commission (CNSC). The CNSC has scheduled two public hearings on the project—one this November and another in February 2026. Upon the successful conclusion of these meetings, the Commission will issue its decision.

If the CNSC grants approval—and we see no immediate reason to expect difficulties—the project can move directly into construction. And it is at that point, we believe, that the real challenges may begin.

The company has stated that it will take just under four years from the start of construction to first production. If the CNSC grants its approval for the Rook project at its February 9th meeting next year, that timeline would imply initial uranium output in early 2030.

We have been closely involved in numerous large-scale mine construction projects over the past thirty-five years, and based on that experience, we believe there is a meaningful risk that Rook’s schedule will slip. This is an enormous undertaking—one that requires not only the development of the mine itself but also the construction of a full ore- and uranium-processing complex.

NexGen is confident it can meet its aggressive timetable. But given the realities of northern Saskatchewan—its climate, its infrastructure limitations, its logistical constraints—we believe holding to that schedule will be extremely difficult. We want to emphasize that any delays should not be interpreted as a reflection on the quality of the project or its management. They would simply be the natural consequence of the complexities inherent in building a mine of this scale.

The last major uranium mine built in Canada was Cameco's Cigar Lake. Construction began in 2005, with first production originally slated for 2007. Instead, the project quickly encountered severe development issues—most notably significant water incursion—that triggered years of delays. Cigar Lake eventually produced its first uranium eight years behind schedule.

The geology at Rook is markedly different from Cigar Lake, and similar flooding problems are not expected. Even so, large-scale mine developments always present unforeseen challenges. Delays are not the exception but the rule, and it would be highly unusual for a project of Rook's scale to proceed without encountering setbacks.

Rook is projected to begin production at 21 million pounds per year, ramping to as much as 30 million pounds—a contribution unmatched by any other new uranium project over the next decade. Any delay in achieving those volumes will further widen the global uranium market's structural deficit, particularly as demand accelerates into the 2030s.

According to the WNA's newly released World Nuclear Fuel Report , uranium demand is set to surge between now and 2040—a conclusion we strongly share, given nuclear power's formidable advantage in producing low-cost, carbon-free electricity. What remains far less clear is where the necessary uranium supply will come from.

Problems are already emerging at several of the world's largest prospective sources of new production, and we believe the structural deficit in global uranium markets is poised to widen sharply in the near term as these supply issues continue to accumulate. We have highlighted two major setbacks that have surfaced recently, and a third—the Rook project—sits plainly on the horizon.

Over the past two years, the positive narrative around uranium has been driven almost entirely by demand. We believe that is about to change. Supply challenges may soon become the dominant force pushing uranium prices materially higher in the short term.

data.title: International Seaways: Strategic Vessel Management And Fundamental Stability Amid Oil Market Volatility
data.description:
It’s been six months since my first analysis of International Seaways, Inc. (INSW). Its value has already risen by over 20%. I understand the market stance despite macroeconomic volatility and geopolitical uncertainty across the globe. But that makes me regret not taking a position on top of some realizations about its valuation. Its topline performance and fundamentals are still robust, which guarantees its strong positioning. Technicals warrant some caution but also show new buying opportunities.

Challenges

Macroeconomic volatility across the globe remains a primary concern for players in the oil and gas industry. For instance, inflation is already at its 14-month high of 3.0%, which means that the YoY price increase has reaccelerated in recent months. This can increase the cost pressures of many businesses, and International Seaways, Inc. is not an exception.

Oil price volatility is another important consideration. While they don’t directly or immediately impact oil tankers, they can influence charter rates and spot rates. Currently, Brent costs $62.89 per barrel, while WTI is $59.44 per barrel. With that, these two oil price benchmarks are very close to their 12-month lows.

This can be a double-edged sword for the company. Why? Lower oil prices can lower the profitability of exploring and producing crude oil. This can impact the demand for oil transportation and tanker rates at some levels. Because INSW has a huge exposure to the spot market, it is also susceptible to volatility risks. As you can see, the spot rate for its Suezmax tankers dropped from $38,044 to $33,310. Meanwhile, the total spot revenue days decreased by 17 days. Its Panamax and MR had lower spot rates and revenue days.

The consolation is that lower oil prices can help INSW lower its fuel expenses. After all, lower crude oil prices also lower the prices of refined products like fuel and gasoline. This was what happened in its most recent performance, considering the decrease in voyage and vessel expenses.

INSW Handles Them Well

Despite these challenges, I believe that INSW is still doing quite well. Although its performance softened last quarter, its strategic operations remained evident. In Q3 2025, its total shipping revenues amounted to $196.4M, down by 12.8% YoY from $225.2M. Its pool revenues remained the primary contributor as they comprised over 70% of the total amount and fell by 14.1% YoY. This should not be surprising considering its high exposure to the spot market. This was slightly offset by time charter revenues, which rose by 6.0% YoY.

INSW offsets the risks by balancing its spot exposure with fixed time charters. This move insulates its operations from market volatility. As you can see, the fixed rates of its crude tankers and product carriers strengthened on a YoY basis despite the lower number of revenue days. So, it’s not surprising that its time and bareboat charter revenues remained resilient. As of October 30, a larger portion of its vessels are subject to fixed rates whether spot or time charter. So, revenues are already locked in regardless of softer market changes this quarter.

Another thing I like about INSW is its well-diversified business model, which offers it some pricing flexibility and margin stability. We know that most of its revenues come from charter hire or its spot market earnings and time charters. But it has specialized services, such as ship-to-ship services. This transfers crude between smaller and larger vessels, especially in parts without or with limited deepwater ports. This is a high-margin revenue stream, so it generates revenues at a manageable cost.

In addition, INSW keeps improving its efficiency through its prudent fleet management. It sells older ships and invests in newer, more fuel-efficient ones like dual-fuel-ready ships. It also invests in line with environmental or IMO standards to ensure its competitiveness in the long run.

This helps it reduce fuel expenses on top of the lower fuel prices. Aside from a more efficient fuel usage, new vessels require less repair and maintenance. As a result, its operating expenses decreased by 5.2%. More interestingly, the percentage of the direct operating expenses decreased to 65.3% versus 67.6%. This means that INSW became less cost-sensitive, which is quite impressive amid stubborn inflation. It was not enough to completely offset the decrease in revenue. But it helped INSW maintain a double-digit operating margin of 40.1%. With that, we can prove that it maintains a well-diversified, high-margin business model.

Another strong point of the company is its robust liquidity. Its operating cash flow remains high enough to cover the whole amount of capex.

INSW complements it with prudent debt management to ensure liquidity. So, it’s not surprising that its cash and cash equivalents remain high and cover the majority of the total assets. In addition, its maturity profile remains well-balanced because over 60% of the total amount will not mature within a year. To be more specific, this portion has a maturity of five years and above. Also, cash and cash equivalents can cover the whole maturing amount of $282.5M in a single payment. This is in line with the Net Debt/EBITDA of only 1.09x, which means that INSW only needs a year to repay the whole borrowings at once even if it doesn’t have to. Hence, INWS maintains a huge capital buffer to sustain its operating capacity and dividends.

On a broader view, I believe that INSW will benefit from the interest rate cut by the Fed. Why? Lower interest rates may also lower the borrowing cost of its variable-rate borrowings. This can help improve its profitability and liquidity. In addition, injecting liquidity may boost economic activity to support consumption and production. As a result, demand for energy commodities may increase. Oil tankers may receive the positive spillovers through a potential increase in spot rates.

Valuation

After revisiting its valuation, I had some realizations about INSW. It’s true that INSW trades at higher price multiples using sales and book value relative to the five-year average. However, the market does not seem to care much about whether the stock is cheaper or more expensive than in previous years. The thing is, the stock price will increase as long as the key financial metrics of the company increase.

To have a more forward-looking approach, I decided to use the Dividend Discount Model. For simplicity, I focused on regular dividends and excluded special payouts. With that, I estimated a dividend growth rate of 10.41%. Meanwhile, I set its risk premium at 7.0%, which is higher than the standard of 5%. That way, I will price in more risks and have a conservative estimation. For its Beta, I simply set it at 1.00 to be consistent with the slope or price direction of the S&P 500. Using all these numbers, I derived a TP or target price of $60.76. If I add a 10% risk consideration, the TP will be $54.68. Hence, its rally is justified and still shows some upside potential.

Technicals

INSW’s technicals are still bullish as the price moves with higher highs and lows above the 200-day SMA. But it warrants some caution as it flattens after the recent downtrend. Note that it’s not below the 50-day SMA, which shows that the current price momentum has weakened relative to the average in the past 50 days. Nonetheless, the 50-day SMA is still trending upwards, which suggests that market sentiments are still optimistic and there is a possibility of a rebound. After all, the flattening of the price trend after the dip shows that selling volume has started to weaken.

Its MACD and RSI can explain it. The MACD line is still trending below the signal line and remains within negative territory along with the histogram. This means that selling volume has outpaced buying volume recently. But the histogram is starting to increase again, which implies that buying volume is also increasing. This can also indicate that buyers found a new support level after the dip. Meanwhile, RSI shows that the dip was prompted by overbuying and profit-taking. Now, the stock is nearly oversold, so buyers may be starting to enter and/or re-enter.

Conclusion

International Seaways, Inc. is not fully insulated from inflationary headwinds and oil market volatility. But its strategic fleet management, well-balanced exposure to spot and time charter rates, well-diversified service offerings, and robust liquidity guarantee sustainability and dividend longevity. Valuation is also realistic, although it’s no longer as attractive as it was in my previous coverage. Technicals are still bullish and show new buying opportunities after the recent dip. I’m upgrading my rating from hold to buy

data.title: Carvana's Comeback: Structural Growth, S&P 500 Inclusion, But Pricey Valuation
data.description:
Carvana (CVNA) is up over 12,000% from its December 2022 lows, from $3.56 to $450 at the time of writing, marking one of the most remarkable turnarounds I've seen from near bankruptcy to now S&P 500 inclusion.

Before I dug into Carvana, I was quite sceptical. Usually, when you see a situation where a stock is up this much without much profitability, you immediately think it's a short. But in the case of Carvana, I came away with more of a neutral outlook, as I think the fundamentals are going to continue getting better, but the valuation is stretched. Perhaps the biggest takeaway for me is that the turnaround that happened is structural (permanent) rather than cyclical (temporary), so this could be the start of better fundamentals for many years to come.

In this write-up, I want to work through the P&L from revenue down to EBIT to make sense of why I think this is a structural change, then what the valuation looks like, plus the impact of S&P 500 inclusion.

Revenue

Q3 2025 revenue came in at $5.647 billion compared to the same quarter the year before, which was $3.655 billion; that's 54.1% YoY growth.

Source

I consider this an idiosyncratic change because this growth came in whilst brick-and-mortar competitors were seeing a decline, and the average sale price has remained stable to up slightly. So this rise in revenue is coming from more volume, all while US used car sales are growing in the low single digits, so it's a sign that there's true idiosyncratic growth in the Carvana brand.

As seen above, if we compare Carvana's current revenue to total US sales, they're only 1.5% of the market right now, so there's reasonable room for growth before any sort of TAM ceiling. Even though going from 1.5% to 4.5% in absolute terms isn't a lot, it's a large change in the economics, as many of the costs are fixed for Carvana, so the actual increase in operating income would be far more than 3x.

COGS

COGS at Carvana consists of four major components:

  • Vehicle acquisition cost (market-priced, largely uncontrollable)
  • Reconditioning costs
  • Logistics and transportation
  • Title, registration, and fulfillment expenses (technically an operating expense, but functionally acts more like per-unit COGS)

Only the vehicle acquisition cost is meaningfully cyclical. The other three are operational and therefore subject to structural improvement from the ADESA acquisition.

Prior to the acquisition, Carvana relied heavily on third-party reconditioning partners. This created three persistent economic problems that were structural disadvantages:

  1. Fragmented quality control, leading to inconsistent reconditioning outcomes and higher downstream costs
  2. Longer cycle times, which increased inventory days on hand and depreciation exposure
  3. Higher per-unit reconditioning costs, driven by third-party margins and inefficiencies

Post-integration, Carvana now controls the entire reconditioning workflow, from intake to inspection, parts sourcing, labour scheduling, and final retail readiness:

The wider margins reflect this change:

Logistics and transportation represent the second major operational COGS lever. Carvana’s logistics network has high fixed costs, including transport infrastructure, routing systems, and labor. As volumes increase within a fixed geographic footprint, the cost per unit declines through density. This is what occurred in 2024 and 2025 as unit volumes increased. In essence, there are economies of scale from the higher volume in a small geographic area, making for lower transportation costs.

Title, registration, and fulfillment costs have also benefited from scale and automation. Carvana has invested heavily in centralized systems that standardize these processes across jurisdictions. Once built, these systems scale sub-linearly with volume. In other words, it's a high fixed cost on the front-end, but that means a lower cost of revenue per vehicle as volume goes up. In the most recent investor presentation, it was highlighted as proprietary tech under the cost structure:

Current gross margins are sitting at 21.37%. Over time the improvements will naturally flatline as there are diminishing returns to how much the company can lower COGS, at which point, it becomes more of a volume game.

Source

Even if gross margins flatline here at around 20%, it's far better than traditional brick and mortar like CarMax (KMX):

Source

Even at its peak, the gross margins for CarMax were 17.6%. So as long as Carvana can keep gross margins above the traditional competition, it will have a price advantage.

Operating Expenses

When it comes to operating expenses, there's operating leverage in Carvana's model. Most operating expenses go to SG&A and don't grow as much as revenue does.

Source

As revenue grew 51.4% YoY, SG&A grew 26.86%.

The company defines SG&A (page 50) as the following:

SG&A expenses include expenses associated with advertising and providing customer service to customers, including financing, title and registration and limited warranty services, operating our vending machines, hubs, physical auctions, logistics and fulfillment network and other corporate overhead expenses, including expenses associated with information technology, product development, engineering, legal, accounting, finance, and business development. SG&A expenses exclude the costs of inspecting and reconditioning vehicles and transporting vehicles from the point of acquisition to the IRC, which are included in cost of sales, and payroll costs for our employees related to the development of software products for internal use, which are capitalized to software and depreciated over the estimated useful lives of the related assets.

We can go down the line of different operating expenses and whether they're fixed or scale with revenue.

The first is advertising, which is discretionary. As revenue grows, I don't expect that advertising will need to grow much, especially as customers start using Carvana as a default. This is semi-fixed.

The second is customer service functions: financing, title, and registration. These costs scale with volume but will become more efficient over time due to process standardisation and automation. I would consider this semi-variable, and as mentioned in the last section, even though it comes up as SG&A on the financials, it acts more like a cost of revenue.

The operation of vending machines, hubs, physical auctions, and the logistics and fulfillment network is often misunderstood as a variable expense. In reality, these assets introduce high fixed costs into SG&A. Vending machines, hubs, and auction facilities require staffing, maintenance, lease expense, and systems support regardless of throughput. Similarly, the logistics and fulfillment network carries fixed costs related to routing infrastructure, scheduling systems, and management oversight. As volume increases, these fixed costs are spread across more units, reducing SG&A per unit. This is why Carvana’s SG&A efficiency improved so rapidly once volumes recovered: the infrastructure was already in place, but underutilised during the downturn. So this is a clear fixed cost.

Corporate overhead—information technology, product development, engineering, legal, accounting, finance, and business development—is fixed.

All in all, I would say SG&A is semi-fixed, and as volumes continue to grow, it will grow at a slower rate. This is where it's pretty easy to see a large operating margin expansion due to operating leverage.

Valuation

Most investors likely think the valuation here is pricey, and I would definitely agree, but I would caution against thinking it's as overvalued as it first looks; nor would I recommend a short position.

The current run rate on EBIT annualises to around $2 billion with an EV of around $102 billion. So, at first glance, it seems clearly overvalued. But as mentioned, there's operating leverage as it grows, so it doesn't take a lot here to get a lower multiple. The aforementioned percentage of the US used car market held by Carvana is 1.5%. It really wouldn't take much to see a tripling to 4.5% in a few years if current revenue growth trends continue.

Say current gross margins stay stable, a 3x in annualised gross profit would be around $13 billion. Since we concluded SG&A is semi-fixed, it will grow, but around half of what the gross profit grows at, which has been the case over the last year. So that means a doubling in SG&A. That makes for an annualised EBIT of around $6.5 billion. This is a multiple against the EV of 15.7x, so still pricey, but once you do this back-of-the-napkin scenario, you can quickly understand how, if this level of growth continues to play out, the valuation might not be as overvalued as it first looks.

Overall, I feel Carvana is overvalued here, and I definitely wouldn't buy it, but at the same time, don't let the headline multiple trick you into thinking it's a clear short thesis.

Flows Over Fundamentals

In terms of what to pay attention to when putting on a trade, I always look at it in terms of the following importance:

  1. Flows
  2. Fundamentals
  3. Valuation

Flows are always at the top. For instance, a company can have great fundamentals, but if there's selling, especially if forced, then it's not a good time to get long and vice versa. A great example of flows over fundamentals is GameStop (GME) in 2021, when the fundamentals weren't great, but positioning was such that the float was more than 140% short, and there was a gamma squeeze to the upside.

As for valuation, I won't buy or sell purely on valuation. Far too many times have I seen companies that look like deep value become value traps, and similarly, high-quality and high-growth companies that trade for high multiples see further multiple expansion.

I bring these points up as Carvana will now be in the S&P 500 starting 22 December 2025, bringing in major flows. I'm writing this on the 20th, so it's likely that by the time you're reading this, you've seen what percentage of the S&P 500 its inclusion is. Based on comparing its float to the total S&P 500 float, I suspect it'll be around a 0.15% allocation.

S&P Global estimates that around $7 trillion in assets mechanically follow the S&P 500. So a 0.15% allocation is $10.5 billion. That's over 10% of the current market cap and 12% of the float, but since prices are set at the margin by short-term participants creating most of the volume, the price swing is multiples of that. Now it's true that there's been buying already in expectation of this event, which will likely sell the news, but nonetheless, this is an upside risk to be aware of.

I mainly write this section to say that although Carvana is likely overvalued, both the flows and better fundamentals aren't bearish, so I wouldn't bet against it.

Investor Takeaways

  • Carvana has had a true structural shift to profitability.
  • As revenue grows, operating leverage will multiply EBIT, and Carvana is at the inflection point for that. Hence, headline multiples are misleading.
  • Valuation is definitely stretched, but perhaps not by as much as it initially seems.
  • S&P 500 inclusion means price-insensitive buying, so I would avoid shorting here.
  • I've put a holding rating here as the underlying company fundamentals are positive, but the valuation here is far too pricey to justify at current fundamentals. It's possible in 2026 we see a significant pullback, at which point Carvana will be on my watch list for a potential long then.

data.title: Fairfax Financial: Value‑Added Acquisitions And Disciplined Investments Are Yielding Rewards
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Bonhoeffer Capital Management Q3 2025 Letter.

Fairfax Financial (FFH:CA) is a global insurance company who uses insurance (via insurance float) to finance a balanced portfolio of developed world fixed income securities and enterprises and global equities. The CEO and the investment team have contacts and relationships in India which provide an insiders advantage for investing in India. Fairfax provides commercial, specialty, workers compensation, Lloyd's of London insurance and re-insurance globally. Fairfax has a decentralized business model, in which it can finance premium growth or contraction in its business lines and geographies. Fairfax has strategically acquired insurance firms as well as enterprises as part of investing its insurance float. About 67% of Fairfax's current premium written has been from insurers acquired since 2009. Fairfax has implemented its underwriting discipline in its acquired insurance firms over time. This is illustrated by the acquired firms combined ratios which have declined from a weighted average of 107% a year after acquisition to 91% in 2024. In addition, Fairfax has 5-year cumulative reserve redundancies of $2 billion on $110 billion in premiums written.

Since 2009, management strategically acquired businesses in direct negotiation with sellers for an average of 1.7 times premium written. Through operational improvements and addition profitable underwriting, the acquired firms were purchased for 0.76 times today's premium written. The businesses are independently operated after acquisition. Acquisitions provide both line of business and geographic areas to re-invest profits and float. As insurance is a cyclical business these acquisitions allow for more efficient allocation of profits and float to niches that have limited capacity at a given time. The business acquisitions are financed primarily by cash or debt which is paid down from cash flow generated by the acquired firms. Over the past ten years, management has acquired three firms. The acquired firms from 2009 to 2017 have ranged in purchase price from $1.3 billion to $4.9 billion.

The investment portfolio is an important part of the profit and book value growth of Fairfax. The portfolio ($67.4 billion) primarily consists of bonds (74%), equities and businesses (26%). Since inception in 1985, this portfolio has generated returns of 7.7% per year and 5.2% over the past 10 years. The bonds are primarily high-grade short duration, with 70% of the bonds having a maturity of less than 3 years and 70% having a credit rating above A. The liability side of the balance sheet includes float ($37.4 billion), debt and preferred stock ($8.0 billion) and common equity ($22 billion). The 10-year cost of float has been -3.2% per year (combined ratio-1) and the current cost of debt and preferred stock is 5.4%. The resulting total cost of liabilities is -1.7%. The equity leverage is 3x (portfolio/common equity). Using the 10-yr average return on investments of 5.2% times leverage (3x) assuming a slight underwriting profit (98.8% combined ratio) results in a RoE 15% (Fairfax's current RoE target).

Fairfax has four levers for earnings growth: 1) buying another insurance firm; 2) expanding underwriting within its existing markets; 3) the growth of the investment portfolio or 4) repurchasing stock. Management is selective in the firms they acquire. When they can't find a suitable acquisition, they can invest in their public equity and private business portfolio. This approach is similar to Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) which has a large public equity and private business portfolio.

Fairfax historically has allowed acquired insurance firms to be run as independent business units but has shared best practices across insurance businesses. Over time, Fairfax has acquired different lines of insurance thus diversifying the cash flows from different insurance businesses. Fairfax began majoracquisitions in 2009 after a period of turning around poorly run previously acquired firms such as Crum & Forester in 1998. In 2009, Fairfax acquired Odyssey Group and The Zenith in 2010. In 2015, Fairfax acquired Brit and Allied World in 2017.

With the integration of the last two acquisitions (Brit (2015) and Allied World (2017)), the 5-yr trailing ROE increased to 12% from 4.6% before these acquisitions. The resulting unlevered RoIIC (see calculation below) has been around 39%, which includes returns from both organic growth initiatives and acquisitions.

Industry Segments

Fairfax competes in numerous insurance markets in North America, Global and International. These businesses have been purchased over the years by Fairfax's management. In 2024, Fairfax generated 27% of revenues and 23% of profit in their North American segment, 53% of revenues and 66% of profit in their Global segment and 20% of revenues and 11% of the profits in their International segment.

Sources of growth for Fairfax include organic and acquisition growth in their insurance segments. Organic growth in these segments is expected to be 3% annual growth rate with any other growth coming from acquisitions (the largest portion of growth historically) or share repurchases.

Fairfax operations have become better over time, as a value-added acquisition strategy and a disciplined investment strategy have yielded rewards. The 5-yr trailing returns on equity have increased from 4.5% in 2017 to 15.9% in 2024. The drivers included consistent combined ratios in trailing 5-yr combined ratios from 94.5% in 2017 to 94.8% in 2024, the increase in 5-yr investment returns from 1.9% in 2017 to 5.4% in 2024 and increases in premiums written from $9.7 billion in 2017 to $31 billion in 2024, a 18% CAGR. The investment leverage was also maintained at 3.0x between 2017 to 2024.

The incremental return on invested capital over the past five years is close to 27%, which has increased Fairfax's RoE over the previous five years. See the calculations below.

Downside Protection

Fairfax's risks include both operational leverage and financial leverage. Operational leverage in the insurance market is based upon the fixed vs. variable costs of the operations. Insurance operations

profitability is based upon underwriting capabilities whose accuracy is dependent upon technology to quantify, monitor and model risk factors and expert underwriters which lead to modestly high fixed costs. These factors lead to economies of scale for the larger operators.

Financial leverage can be measured by the investment leverage ratio. Fairfax has a modest investment leverage ratio of 2.8 versus other insurance firms and lower than Fairfax's history. The history and projected financial performance for Fairfax is illustrated below.

Management and Incentives

Fairfax's management team has developed an M&A engine and operationally efficient firms in profitable niches of the insurance industry. They perform acquisitions when targets are available at the right price. At times, the acquisitions are financed by equity and capital is returned to shareholders via buybacks when there are not opportunities to invest organically or via M&A.

The base compensation for the management team (defined as the top three executives except the CEO) is roughly the same amount of $750k to $1.25 million per year. The CEO has modest compensation of $600k and also owns about 6.7% of Fairfax ($2.6 billion). The next top three executives have the highest total compensation, $10.6 million collectively, primarily due to performance-based compensation and share-based compensation. Over the past three years, their total compensation was about $14 million per year, about 0.36% of net income per year. The CEO and executives currently hold 2.1 million shares and options (8.8% of shares outstanding worth $3.4 billion), which is more than 300 times their 2024 salaries and bonuses of $10.6 million. Non-options-based incentive compensation for the executive team is set and approved annually by the board of directors. Over the past two years, 0.75% shares outstanding per year were issued to employees.

Board members have a significant investment in Fairfax. The board and management owns about 9% of total shares outstanding.

Valuation

The key to the valuation of Fairfax is the expected growth rate. The current valuation implies an earnings increase of 0% in perpetuity using the Graham formula ((8.5 + 2g)). The historical 10-year earnings growth has been 23% per year including acquisitions and the current return on equity of 8%.

A bottom-up analysis based upon market growth rates of Fairfax's markets' (insurance and re-insurance) results was used to estimate an organic growth rate of 3% for Fairfax. This does not include any future acquisitions. If we include 1% growth for acquisitions, then the base revenue growth rate is 4%. Incorporating operational leverage and buybacks results in 8% EPS growth rate. Historically, Fairfax's EPS growth rate was 8% per year including three acquisitions over 10 years. If we assume no acquisitions over the next seven years the forward EPS of 7% growth per year is conservative. Using a 7% expected growth rate, the resulting current multiple is 23x of earnings, while Fairfax trades at an earnings multiple of 9x. If we look at insurance comparables, which are larger but have slower growth prospects, they have an average earnings multiple of 15x. If we apply 15x earnings to Fairfax's estimated 2026 earnings of $215, then we arrive at a value of $3,225 per share, which is a reasonable short-term target. If we use a 7% seven-year growth rate, then we arrive at a value of $4,520 per share. This results in a four-year IRR of 27%.

Growth Framework

Another way to look at growth and the valuation of companies is to estimate the EPS four years into the future and see how much of today's price incorporates this growth. Using the same revenue described above results in a 2029 of $266.20, or 6.5x the current price. If we assume a steady-state growth rate from 2030 on of 7%, then this results in a fair value Graham multiple of 22.5x or $5,989 per share, similar to the four-year-forward valuation above of $4,520 per share.

Comparables and Benchmarking

Below are comparable insurance firms (high RoE broad-based insurers). Compared to these firms, Fairfax has investment returns on the high end of the range and has underwriting results on the low end of the range (but still positive) and a below-average multiple. Fairfax also has an above average 5-year RoEs (including the up part of the recent underwriting cycle) and one of the highest ten-year BV + dividend growth rates. 10-year BV + dividend yield was examined to look at growth over an entire underwriting cycle.

Risks

The primary risks are:

  • slower-than-expected acquisition growth;
  • lower-than-expected growth in Fairfax's insurance end markets; and
  • a lack of new investment opportunities (mergers and acquisitions) coupled with higher stock prices making buybacks less accretive.

Potential Upside/Catalyst

The primary catalysts are:

  • higher-than-expected acquisition growth;
  • faster growth in Fairfax's insurance end markets; and
  • increased local scope or purchase of local scale in new markets.

Timeline/Investment Horizon

The short-term target is $3,225 per share, which is almost 88% above today's stock price. Assuming a modest 7% per year earnings per share growth over the next five years, then a value of $5,254 per share. $5,254 is the midpoint of the two methods described above and would result in a 25% IRR over the next five years.

Disclaimer

This letter does not contain all the information that is material to a prospective investor in the Bonhoeffer Fund, L.P. (the "Fund").

Not an Offer: The information set forth in this letter is being made available to generally describe the philosophies of the Fund. The letter does not constitute an offer, solicitation or recommendation to sell or an offer to buy any securities, investment products or investment advisory services. Such an offer may only be made to accredited investors by means of delivery of a confidential private placement memorandum, or other similar materials that contain a description of material terms relating to such investment. The information published and the opinions expressed herein are provided for informational purposes only.

No Advice: Nothing contained herein constitutes financial, legal, tax, or other advice. The Fund makes no representation that the information and opinions expressed herein are accurate, complete or current. The information contained herein is current as of the date hereof but may become outdated or change.

Risks: An investment in the Fund is speculative due to a variety of risks and considerations as detailed in the Confidential Private Placement Memorandum of the Fund, and this letter is qualified in its entirety by the more complete information contained therein and in the related subscription materials.

No Recommendation: The mention of or reference to specific companies, strategies or instruments in this letter should not be interpreted as a recommendation or opinion that you should make any purchase or sale or participate in any transaction.
